cards.front: What is the closed-loop gain formula for negative feedback?
cards.back: A_f = A / (1 + Aβ), where A is open-loop gain and β is the feedback factor. The gain is divided by D = 1+Aβ.

cards.front: What is the loop gain T?
cards.back: T = Aβ — the product of open-loop amplifier gain A and feedback factor β. It determines how strongly feedback controls the system.

cards.front: What is the desensitivity factor D?
cards.back: D = 1 + Aβ. All feedback effects scale with D: gain ÷ D, sensitivity ÷ D, distortion ÷ D, bandwidth × D.

cards.front: When Aβ >> 1, what does A_f simplify to?
cards.back: A_f ≈ 1/β. The closed-loop gain depends only on the feedback network (stable passive components), not the amplifier gain.

cards.front: How is the feedback factor β defined in a feedback system?
cards.back: β = X_f / X_o — the ratio of feedback signal to output signal. β is a property of the feedback network only, not the amplifier.

cards.front: What is the error signal X_i in a negative feedback system?
cards.back: X_i = X_S - X_f. The feedback signal is subtracted from the source to produce an error (difference) signal that drives the amplifier.

cards.front: How does transistor β (h_FE) differ from feedback network β?
cards.back: Transistor h_FE = I_C/I_B (typically 50–300, a current gain). Feedback β = X_f/X_o (typically << 1, set by resistors). Same symbol, completely different.

cards.front: What happens when Aβ = -1 (positive feedback condition)?
cards.back: Closed-loop gain → ∞ and the amplifier oscillates (1+Aβ = 0, so A_f = A/0). This is the Barkhausen criterion used deliberately in oscillator design.

cards.front: Why does negative feedback stabilize gain against transistor variations?
cards.back: dA_f/A_f = (1/D) × dA/A. Fractional gain change is reduced by factor D = 1+Aβ. β is set by stable resistors, not variable transistors.

cards.front: What is the purpose of complementary NPN/PNP pairs in an amplifier?
cards.back: Their VBE temperature coefficients are opposite in sign, so they cancel each other's temperature drift. Improves DC stability before feedback is even applied.

cards.front: Derive A_f = A/(1+Aβ) from the block diagram equations.
cards.back: X_o = A·X_i = A(X_S - X_f) = A(X_S - βX_o). So X_o(1+Aβ) = AX_S. Therefore A_f = X_o/X_S = A/(1+Aβ).

cards.front: If A = 200 and β = 0.05, what are T, D, and A_f?
cards.back: T = Aβ = 200×0.05 = 10. D = 1+T = 11. A_f = A/D = 200/11 ≈ 18.2. Since Aβ = 10 >> 1, A_f ≈ 1/β = 20 (close approximation).
